hook: You already know how to describe curves in 2D using functions, and how vectors capture direction and magnitude in space. But how do you describe the path of a spacecraft, the spin of a roller coaster, or the orbit of a planet — all at once, in 3D? This unit gives you exactly that power.
concept: The unifying idea is that by packaging $x$, $y$, and $z$ into a single vector-valued function $\mathbf{r}(t)$, you can analyze motion and curves in three dimensions using the same calculus tools you already know — formally captured by the concept of a *smooth parametric curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to define and graph curves in 3D space by expressing position as a vector-valued function $\mathbf{r}(t) = \langle f(t), g(t), h(t) \rangle$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Derivatives and Integrals of Vector Functions
topic_roadmap.description: Master differentiating and integrating vector-valued functions component-by-component to find tangent vectors and recover position from velocity or acceleration.
Apply integration to measure the length of a space curve and use the curvature $\kappa$ to quantify how sharply the curve bends at any point.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Motion in Space: Velocity and Acceleration
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to interpret the derivatives of a position function as the velocity and acceleration vectors of a moving object in three-dimensional space.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Tangential and Normal Components of Acceleration (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Decompose the acceleration vector into a component along the path (speeding up or slowing down) and a component perpendicular to it (changing direction), revealing the geometry of any motion.
Apply vector calculus to derive Kepler's three laws of planetary orbits, connecting the mathematics of curves in space to the real gravitational physics of our solar system.
